Version en espanol
Made with MyST

12. Multicollinearity, variable selection, and validation

MATH 4210, Chapter 12

Espanol

Esta leccion esta disponible en espanol: Leer en espanol

Measuring body fat well is a nuisance. The accurate way is to weigh a person underwater and work out their density, which needs a tank, a technician, and a cooperative subject. The cheap way is a tape measure. So a natural question follows: can a handful of tape measurements (waist, chest, thigh, wrist, and the rest) predict the percent body fat that the underwater weighing gives? A 1985 study recorded both for 252 men, and that dataset, fat.csv, is the one this chapter works with from start to finish. (This is the larger 252-man study with thirteen measurements, not the three-measurement, twenty-man body-fat table you met for extra sums of squares in 8.5 Added-variable plots; the two share a “thigh” column but are different data.)

There is an obvious plan: regress body fat on all thirteen measurements and read off the coefficients. When you do, the model explains about 75 percent of the variation, which sounds like a success. Then you look at the coefficients and something is wrong. The coefficient on weight is negative, as if heavier men had less fat once you hold the other measures fixed. Several predictors that everyone knows matter come out statistically insignificant. Drop one measurement and the others lurch. The problem is that the tape measurements are all measuring roughly the same thing, body size, so they are tangled together. Figure 1 shows just how tangled.

This chapter is about living with too many predictors, most of them redundant. Three questions organize it. First, how do we diagnose and describe the damage that correlated predictors do, using the variance inflation factor? Second, when several models are on the table, how do we choose, using criteria like Mallows CpC_pCp​ and cross-validation that reward prediction rather than mere fit? Third, what modern tools (the lasso and ridge regression) sidestep the whole selection problem by shrinking coefficients instead of deleting them? Along the way you will meet the most important warning in applied statistics: a model chosen to fit your data will always look better on that data than it deserves.

This lesson at a glance

  • What we are doing: Living with many, largely redundant predictors on the body-fat data: diagnosing multicollinearity with the variance inflation factor, choosing a model with CpC_pCp​, AIC, BIC, and cross-validation, and shrinking with ridge and lasso.

  • Why we are doing it: Regress body fat on all thirteen tape measurements and the coefficients go haywire (weight comes out negative) because they all measure body size; worse, any model chosen to fit its own data looks better on that data than it deserves.

  • Main objective: Diagnose collinearity with the VIF, compare selection criteria, run kkk-fold cross-validation by hand, and describe ridge and lasso shrinkage.

  • What changed from the last chapters: Chapters 8 through 11 gave you one multiple regression and the tools to interpret and diagnose it; from here you face many competing models and judge them by out-of-sample prediction rather than in-sample fit, iterating the FIT and CHECK stages of The modeling workflow together.

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

  • Diagnose multicollinearity with the variance inflation factor, and derive the VIF from an auxiliary regression.

  • Explain what multicollinearity does and does not harm, and list the standard remedies.

  • Compute and compare model-selection criteria: adjusted R2R^2R2, Mallows CpC_pCp​, AIC, BIC, and PRESS.

  • Derive Mallows CpC_pCp​ and prove the PRESS deleted-residual shortcut.

  • Explain, bluntly, why stepwise selection and post-selection inference mislead.

  • Implement kkk-fold cross-validation by hand in R and Python.

  • Describe ridge and lasso shrinkage and demonstrate them on the body-fat data.

12.1 Multicollinearity and the variance inflation factor¶

Intuition¶

Chapters 7 and 8 built a multiple regression and read each coefficient as a partial slope, the effect of one predictor with the others held fixed (8.5 Added-variable plots). That reading quietly assumed the predictors carried separate information. The vignette showed the damage when they do not; now we name the cause, multicollinearity (Definition 12.1).

Definition 12.1: Multicollinearity

Multicollinearity is correlation among the predictors of a regression. It inflates the variance of the individual coefficients without biasing them, and does not harm predictions made inside the range of the data.

A little of it is harmless and normal. A lot is trouble, because the model cannot tell the correlated predictors apart. Imagine two predictors that move almost in lockstep, like weight and hip circumference. The data show what happens to body fat when both rise together, but they show almost nothing about what happens when one rises and the other stays fixed, because that combination barely occurs. So the model has no firm basis for splitting the shared effect between them. It can give weight a big positive coefficient and hip a big negative one, or the reverse, and fit the data about equally well either way. Figure 2 draws the picture.

Key idea

Collinearity is a variance problem, not a bias problem. It leaves the coefficients correct on average and predictions inside the data range intact; what it damages is the precision of the individual coefficients, whose standard errors balloon, whose signs flip from sample to sample, and whose ttt tests go quiet. Trust a collinear model’s predictions, distrust its coefficients.

The variance inflation factor (Definition 12.2) measures exactly how much the variance is inflated.

The full model, and why its coefficients misbehave¶

Fit the full model first, so the symptoms are concrete. The response is brozek, percent body fat by Brozek’s formula, and the thirteen predictors are the tape and scale measurements. Recall from 4.2 Correlation and the regression slope that for a single predictor, R2R^2R2 is just the squared correlation; with many predictors R2R^2R2 keeps climbing as you add columns, whether or not they help, so a high R2R^2R2 alone proves nothing.

Example 12.1: The full body-fat model and its unstable coefficients

Question. Fit body fat on all thirteen measurements. Does the fit look good, and do the coefficients make sense?

Intuition. A large R2R^2R2 can coexist with nonsensical individual coefficients when predictors are redundant. Read both.

Formula. The general linear model Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε with p=14p = 14p=14 parameters (thirteen predictors plus the intercept) and n=252n = 252n=252 men, fit by least squares b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

Computation.

fat <- read.csv("data/fat.csv")
dim(fat)
preds <- c("age", "weight", "height", "neck", "chest", "abdom", "hip",
           "thigh", "knee", "ankle", "biceps", "forearm", "wrist")
full <- lm(brozek ~ age + weight + height + neck + chest + abdom + hip +
             thigh + knee + ankle + biceps + forearm + wrist, data = fat)
round(coef(summary(full)), 4)
[1] 252  18
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -15.2925    16.0699 -0.9516   0.3423
age           0.0568     0.0300  1.8951   0.0593
weight       -0.0803     0.0496 -1.6198   0.1066
height       -0.0646     0.0889 -0.7264   0.4683
neck         -0.4375     0.2153 -2.0319   0.0433
chest        -0.0236     0.0918 -0.2570   0.7974
abdom         0.8854     0.0801 11.0572   0.0000
hip          -0.1984     0.1352 -1.4681   0.1434
thigh         0.2319     0.1337  1.7342   0.0842
knee         -0.0117     0.2241 -0.0521   0.9585
ankle         0.1635     0.2051  0.7972   0.4261
biceps        0.1528     0.1585  0.9640   0.3360
forearm       0.4305     0.1845  2.3339   0.0204
wrist        -1.4765     0.4955 -2.9798   0.0032
c(R2 = summary(full)$r.squared,
  adjR2 = summary(full)$adj.r.squared,
  s = summary(full)$sigma)
       R2     adjR2         s
0.7489799 0.7352688 3.9879734

Now the same fit in Python with statsmodels.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

fat = pd.read_csv("data/fat.csv")
print(fat.shape)
preds = ["age", "weight", "height", "neck", "chest", "abdom", "hip",
         "thigh", "knee", "ankle", "biceps", "forearm", "wrist"]
full = smf.ols("brozek ~ " + " + ".join(preds), data=fat).fit()
coefs = pd.DataFrame({"coef": full.params, "se": full.bse,
                      "t": full.tvalues, "p": full.pvalues})
print(coefs.round(4))
(252, 18)
              coef       se        t       p
Intercept -15.2925  16.0699  -0.9516  0.3423
age         0.0568   0.0300   1.8951  0.0593
weight     -0.0803   0.0496  -1.6198  0.1066
height     -0.0646   0.0889  -0.7264  0.4683
neck       -0.4375   0.2153  -2.0319  0.0433
chest      -0.0236   0.0918  -0.2570  0.7974
abdom       0.8854   0.0801  11.0572  0.0000
hip        -0.1984   0.1352  -1.4681  0.1434
thigh       0.2319   0.1337   1.7342  0.0842
knee       -0.0117   0.2241  -0.0521  0.9585
ankle       0.1635   0.2051   0.7972  0.4261
biceps      0.1528   0.1585   0.9640  0.3360
forearm     0.4305   0.1845   2.3339  0.0204
wrist      -1.4765   0.4955  -2.9798  0.0032
print(round(full.rsquared, 4), round(full.rsquared_adj, 4),
      round(np.sqrt(full.scale), 4))
0.749 0.7353 3.988

Interpretation. The model explains R2=0.749R^2 = 0.749R2=0.749 of the variation in body fat, and the typical prediction error is s=3.99s = 3.99s=3.99 percentage points. That looks fine. The coefficients do not. Weight has a negative coefficient, which is absurd on its own: heavier men, holding tape measures fixed, are not leaner. Chest, hip, knee, and several others are far from significant, even though body size clearly relates to fat. Only the abdomen (waist) measurement stands out sharply (t=11.06t = 11.06t=11.06). The trouble is not that these predictors are useless; it is that they are redundant, so the model cannot assign credit among them. This is multicollinearity, and the next tool measures it.

You saw a small version of this in 8.5 Added-variable plots, where a twenty-man body-fat table had a triceps and a thigh measurement so correlated that their coefficients turned unstable. Here the same disease appears at full scale, with thirteen tangled predictors instead of two. A quick look at the correlations among the size measurements confirms the diagnosis.

round(cor(fat[, c("weight", "chest", "abdom", "hip", "thigh")]), 3)
       weight chest abdom   hip thigh
weight  1.000 0.894 0.888 0.941 0.869
chest   0.894 1.000 0.916 0.829 0.730
abdom   0.888 0.916 1.000 0.874 0.767
hip     0.941 0.829 0.874 1.000 0.896
thigh   0.869 0.730 0.767 0.896 1.000
print(fat[["weight", "chest", "abdom", "hip", "thigh"]].corr().round(3))
        weight  chest  abdom    hip  thigh
weight   1.000  0.894  0.888  0.941  0.869
chest    0.894  1.000  0.916  0.829  0.730
abdom    0.888  0.916  1.000  0.874  0.767
hip      0.941  0.829  0.874  1.000  0.896
thigh    0.869  0.730  0.767  0.896  1.000

Every pair correlates above 0.7, and weight with hip reaches 0.941. These columns are near-duplicates of one another.

Formula¶

Definition 12.2: Variance inflation factor

For predictor XkX_kXk​, the variance inflation factor is

VIFk=11−Rk2,\mathrm{VIF}_k = \frac{1}{1 - R_k^2},VIFk​=1−Rk2​1​,

where Rk2R_k^2Rk2​ is the coefficient of determination from the auxiliary regression of XkX_kXk​ on all the other predictors. It is the factor by which collinearity multiplies the variance of bkb_kbk​.

Definition 12.3: Auxiliary regression

The auxiliary regression for predictor XkX_kXk​ is the least-squares regression of XkX_kXk​ on all the other predictors. Its coefficient of determination Rk2R_k^2Rk2​ measures how completely the others reproduce XkX_kXk​, and feeds the VIF through VIFk=1/(1−Rk2)\mathrm{VIF}_k = 1/(1 - R_k^2)VIFk​=1/(1−Rk2​).

In words: regress predictor kkk on the remaining predictors, see how well they predict it, and VIFk\mathrm{VIF}_kVIFk​ is one over the leftover fraction. If XkX_kXk​ is unrelated to the others, Rk2=0R_k^2 = 0Rk2​=0 and VIFk=1\mathrm{VIF}_k = 1VIFk​=1. If the others explain 90 percent of it, Rk2=0.9R_k^2 = 0.9Rk2​=0.9 and VIFk=10\mathrm{VIF}_k = 10VIFk​=10. A common rule of thumb flags VIFk>10\mathrm{VIF}_k > 10VIFk​>10 (equivalently Rk2>0.9R_k^2 > 0.9Rk2​>0.9) as serious, though the cutoff is a convention, not a law.

The shape of that formula is worth a look, because it explains why a little collinearity is harmless and a lot is a disaster. Figure 3 plots the VIF against Rk2R_k^2Rk2​. The curve is nearly flat while a predictor is mostly its own, then bends sharply upward as the others close in on reproducing it. Weight, which the other twelve measurements explain 97 percent of, sits far up the steep part.

The name is literal. The VIF is the factor by which the variance of bkb_kbk​ is multiplied, compared with the variance it would have if XkX_kXk​ were uncorrelated with the other predictors:

Var⁡{bk}=σ2Skk (1−Rk2)=σ2Skk⋅VIFk,Skk=∑i=1n(Xik−Xˉk)2.\operatorname{Var}\{b_k\} = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}\,(1 - R_k^2)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}} \cdot \mathrm{VIF}_k, \qquad S_{kk} = \sum_{i=1}^n (X_{ik} - \bar{X}_k)^2 .Var{bk​}=Skk​(1−Rk2​)σ2​=Skk​σ2​⋅VIFk​,Skk​=i=1∑n​(Xik​−Xˉk​)2.

In words: the variance of a coefficient is the usual σ2/Skk\sigma^2 / S_{kk}σ2/Skk​ (small when the predictor is spread wide) times the inflation factor. Collinearity enters only through that last factor.

Derivation (the VIF from the auxiliary regression)¶

Theorem 12.4: Variance of a least-squares coefficient

Under the general linear model, the coefficient bkb_kbk​ of predictor XkX_kXk​ has variance

Var⁡{bk}=σ2Skk (1−Rk2)=σ2Skk⋅VIFk,Skk=∑i=1n(Xik−Xˉk)2,\operatorname{Var}\{b_k\} = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}\,(1 - R_k^2)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}} \cdot \mathrm{VIF}_k, \qquad S_{kk} = \sum_{i=1}^n (X_{ik} - \bar{X}_k)^2,Var{bk​}=Skk​(1−Rk2​)σ2​=Skk​σ2​⋅VIFk​,Skk​=i=1∑n​(Xik​−Xˉk​)2,

where Rk2R_k^2Rk2​ is the auxiliary R2R^2R2 of XkX_kXk​ on the other predictors. Collinearity enters only through the factor VIFk=1/(1−Rk2)\mathrm{VIF}_k = 1/(1 - R_k^2)VIFk​=1/(1−Rk2​).

Proof. Fix a predictor XkX_kXk​. By the added-variable construction of 8.5 Added-variable plots, the least-squares coefficient bkb_kbk​ equals the slope from a simple regression of YYY on the part of XkX_kXk​ that the other predictors do not explain. Make that part concrete: run the auxiliary regression of XkX_kXk​ on all the other predictors, and call its residuals X~ik\tilde{X}_{ik}X~ik​. These residuals are XkX_kXk​ with the other predictors’ information removed.

The added-variable result says bk=∑iX~ikYi/∑iX~ik2b_k = \sum_i \tilde{X}_{ik} Y_i / \sum_i \tilde{X}_{ik}^2bk​=∑i​X~ik​Yi​/∑i​X~ik2​, a simple-regression slope with the X~ik\tilde{X}_{ik}X~ik​ as the predictor. From 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem, a simple-regression slope ∑kiYi\sum k_i Y_i∑ki​Yi​ with weights ki=X~ik/∑jX~jk2k_i = \tilde{X}_{ik} / \sum_j \tilde{X}_{jk}^2ki​=X~ik​/∑j​X~jk2​ has variance σ2∑iki2=σ2/∑iX~ik2\sigma^2 \sum_i k_i^2 = \sigma^2 / \sum_i \tilde{X}_{ik}^2σ2∑i​ki2​=σ2/∑i​X~ik2​. So

Var⁡{bk}=σ2∑iX~ik2.\operatorname{Var}\{b_k\} = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{i} \tilde{X}_{ik}^2} .Var{bk​}=∑i​X~ik2​σ2​.

Now identify the denominator. The auxiliary regression has total sum of squares ∑i(Xik−Xˉk)2=Skk\sum_i (X_{ik} - \bar{X}_k)^2 = S_{kk}∑i​(Xik​−Xˉk​)2=Skk​ and error sum of squares ∑iX~ik2\sum_i \tilde{X}_{ik}^2∑i​X~ik2​. By the definition of R2R^2R2 applied to that auxiliary regression,

Rk2=1−∑iX~ik2Skk⟹∑iX~ik2=Skk (1−Rk2).R_k^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_i \tilde{X}_{ik}^2}{S_{kk}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sum_i \tilde{X}_{ik}^2 = S_{kk}\,(1 - R_k^2) .Rk2​=1−Skk​∑i​X~ik2​​⟹i∑​X~ik2​=Skk​(1−Rk2​).

Substitute:

Var⁡{bk}=σ2Skk (1−Rk2)=σ2Skk⋅11−Rk2.\operatorname{Var}\{b_k\} = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}\,(1 - R_k^2)} = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - R_k^2} .Var{bk​}=Skk​(1−Rk2​)σ2​=Skk​σ2​⋅1−Rk2​1​.

The first factor σ2/Skk\sigma^2 / S_{kk}σ2/Skk​ is what the variance would be if XkX_kXk​ were free of the others (Rk2=0R_k^2 = 0Rk2​=0). The second factor 1/(1−Rk2)=VIFk1/(1 - R_k^2) = \mathrm{VIF}_k1/(1−Rk2​)=VIFk​ is the price of collinearity. When the other predictors nearly reproduce XkX_kXk​, Rk2→1R_k^2 \to 1Rk2​→1 and the variance explodes. ■\blacksquare■

R and Python¶

R’s car package computes every VIF at once. The formula above lets you check any one of them by hand from a single auxiliary regression.

Example 12.2: Variance inflation factors for the body-fat model

Question. Which predictors are the worst offenders, and can we reproduce one VIF from its auxiliary regression?

Intuition. A VIF near 1 is clean; a VIF above 10 means that predictor is more than 90 percent explained by the others. Compute all thirteen, then rebuild the weight VIF by hand.

Formula. VIFk=1/(1−Rk2)\mathrm{VIF}_k = 1/(1 - R_k^2)VIFk​=1/(1−Rk2​), with Rk2R_k^2Rk2​ from regressing XkX_kXk​ on the rest.

Computation.

library(car)
round(vif(full), 2)
    age  weight  height    neck   chest   abdom     hip   thigh    knee   ankle
   2.25   33.51    1.67    4.32    9.46   11.77   14.80    7.78    4.61    1.91
 biceps forearm   wrist
   3.62    2.19    3.38
aux <- lm(weight ~ age + height + neck + chest + abdom + hip + thigh +
            knee + ankle + biceps + forearm + wrist, data = fat)
R2_weight <- summary(aux)$r.squared
c(R2_weight = R2_weight, VIF_weight = 1 / (1 - R2_weight))
 R2_weight VIF_weight
 0.9701576 33.5093198
def vif_by_hand(data, predictors):
    out = {}
    for v in predictors:
        others = [p for p in predictors if p != v]
        r2 = smf.ols(v + " ~ " + " + ".join(others), data=data).fit().rsquared
        out[v] = 1 / (1 - r2)
    return pd.Series(out)

print(vif_by_hand(fat, preds).round(2))
age         2.25
weight     33.51
height      1.67
neck        4.32
chest       9.46
abdom      11.77
hip        14.80
thigh       7.78
knee        4.61
ankle       1.91
biceps      3.62
forearm     2.19
wrist       3.38
dtype: float64
others = [p for p in preds if p != "weight"]
r2_weight = smf.ols("weight ~ " + " + ".join(others), data=fat).fit().rsquared
print(round(r2_weight, 4), round(1 / (1 - r2_weight), 3))
0.9702 33.509

Interpretation. Weight has VIF=33.5\mathrm{VIF} = 33.5VIF=33.5: the other twelve measurements explain Rk2=0.970R_k^2 = 0.970Rk2​=0.970 of it. So its coefficient’s variance is inflated 33-fold, and its standard error is 33.5≈5.8\sqrt{33.5} \approx 5.833.5​≈5.8 times what it would be if weight stood alone. Hip (14.8) and abdomen (11.8) are also badly inflated. Age (2.25), height (1.67), and ankle (1.91) are almost clean. Figure 4 shows the ranking. This is why the weight coefficient came out negative and insignificant in Example 12.1: it is measured against a backdrop of near-duplicates.

Remedies¶

There are four honest responses to high VIFs. First, do nothing, if you only care about prediction inside the range of the data: collinearity does not bias Y^\hat{Y}Y^ or widen its interval much, so a predictive model can carry redundant predictors safely. Second, drop predictors: if weight, hip, and abdomen say nearly the same thing, keep the one you can measure best and discard the rest. Third, combine them into a single index (an average, or a principal component) that captures the shared size signal. Fourth, shrink the coefficients with ridge regression (Section 12.5), which tolerates collinearity by design. What you must not do is read a collinear coefficient as a real causal effect and act on its sign.

Durable skill: Distrust a coefficient before you interpret it

A coefficient is a claim about what happens when one predictor changes and the others hold still. If the data almost never held the others still (because the predictors move together), that claim rests on almost nothing, no matter how confidently the software prints it. Before you tell anyone “each extra kilogram lowers body fat,” check whether the data could even see that comparison. The habit of asking “does my data contain the contrast this coefficient describes?” will save you from confident nonsense in every regression you ever run, and in every regression an AI assistant hands you.

Try it 12.1

A colleague reports a VIF of 1.0 for one predictor and 25 for another, and concludes the second predictor is “more important.” Correct the reasoning, and say what a VIF of 25 implies about the auxiliary Rk2R_k^2Rk2​.

Solution

The VIF says nothing about importance; it measures redundancy. A VIF of 1.0 means the predictor is uncorrelated with the others, so its coefficient is estimated cleanly. A VIF of 25 means the other predictors explain Rk2=1−1/25=0.96R_k^2 = 1 - 1/25 = 0.96Rk2​=1−1/25=0.96 of that predictor, so its coefficient is poorly estimated (variance inflated 25-fold), the opposite of a reason to trust it. A high-VIF predictor may be very important or completely useless; the VIF cannot tell you which. It only warns that this particular coefficient is unstable.

12.2 Choosing among models: selection criteria¶

Intuition¶

The full model has redundant predictors; a smaller model might predict just as well and be easier to trust. But which smaller model? With thirteen predictors there are 213=81922^{13} = 8192213=8192 possible subsets. We need a score that ranks them. The obvious score, R2R^2R2, is useless for this, because R2R^2R2 never decreases when you add a predictor: the biggest model always wins, even if the added predictors are noise. Recall from 4.2 Correlation and the regression slope that R2R^2R2 is squared correlation, a measure of fit, not of prediction. A good selection criterion must instead reward fit while charging a penalty for each extra parameter, so that a predictor earns its place only if it pays for itself.

Four criteria do this, each in its own currency. Adjusted R2R^2R2 discounts R2R^2R2 by the degrees of freedom spent. Mallows CpC_pCp​ estimates the total prediction error and compares it to the number of parameters. AIC and BIC trade goodness of fit against a size penalty from information theory. PRESS predicts each case from a model fit without it. They usually agree on the broad message (drop the deadweight) while disagreeing on the exact size, which is itself a lesson: there is no single correct model.

Formula¶

Let a candidate model have ppp parameters (predictors plus intercept), error sum of squares SSEp=∑i(Yi−Y^i)2\mathrm{SSE}_p = \sum_i (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2SSEp​=∑i​(Yi​−Y^i​)2, and SSTO=∑i(Yi−Yˉ)2\mathrm{SSTO} = \sum_i (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2SSTO=∑i​(Yi​−Yˉ)2. Let σ^2\hat{\sigma}^2σ^2 be the mean square error of the largest (full) model, our best estimate of the noise variance σ2\sigma^2σ2. The four criteria are

Ra,p2=1−SSEp/(n−p)SSTO/(n−1),Cp=SSEpσ^2−(n−2p),R^2_{a,p} = 1 - \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p/(n - p)}{\mathrm{SSTO}/(n-1)}, \qquad C_p = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{\hat{\sigma}^2} - (n - 2p),Ra,p2​=1−SSTO/(n−1)SSEp​/(n−p)​,Cp​=σ^2SSEp​​−(n−2p),
AICp=nln⁡ ⁣(SSEpn)+2p,BICp=nln⁡ ⁣(SSEpn)+pln⁡n.\mathrm{AIC}_p = n \ln\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{n}\right) + 2p, \qquad \mathrm{BIC}_p = n \ln\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{n}\right) + p \ln n .AICp​=nln(nSSEp​​)+2p,BICp​=nln(nSSEp​​)+plnn.

  • Ra,p2R^2_{a,p}Ra,p2​ (adjusted R2R^2R2): like R2R^2R2 but with SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE and SSTO\mathrm{SSTO}SSTO divided by their degrees of freedom. Adding a useless predictor lowers it. Bigger is better.

  • CpC_pCp​ (Mallows): small is better; a model with no bias has Cp≈pC_p \approx pCp​≈p, so you look for models with low CpC_pCp​ that also sit near the line Cp=pC_p = pCp​=p.

  • AICp\mathrm{AIC}_pAICp​, BICp\mathrm{BIC}_pBICp​: fit term plus penalty; smaller is better. BIC’s penalty pln⁡np \ln nplnn is heavier than AIC’s 2p2p2p whenever n>7n > 7n>7, so BIC prefers smaller models.

That last claim is easy to see in a picture. Figure 5 draws the two size penalties as straight lines. With n=252n = 252n=252 men, BIC charges about ln⁡252≈5.5\ln 252 \approx 5.5ln252≈5.5 for each extra parameter while AIC charges only 2, so the BIC line climbs almost three times as steeply. A predictor has to improve the fit more to pay off its heavier BIC toll, which is exactly why BIC lands on smaller models.

The fifth criterion, PRESS, gets its own subsection because it measures something different: honest out-of-sample error.

PRESSp=∑i=1n(Yi−Y^(i))2,\mathrm{PRESS}_p = \sum_{i=1}^n \bigl(Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)}\bigr)^2,PRESSp​=i=1∑n​(Yi​−Y^(i)​)2,

where Y^(i)\hat{Y}_{(i)}Y^(i)​ is the prediction for case iii from the model refit with case iii removed. In words: hide each observation, predict it from the rest, and add up the squared misses. Smaller is better, and unlike SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, PRESS cannot be driven down just by adding predictors, because a junk predictor helps the fit but hurts the left-out prediction.

A note on AIC and BIC constants

Different software adds different constants to AIC and BIC. R’s AIC() counts the variance as an extra estimated parameter and includes the 2π2\pi2π term from the normal likelihood; statsmodels uses a slightly different constant. The absolute numbers therefore differ between R and Python, but the differences between models within one program, which are all that matter for selection, agree. Compare AIC values only within a single language.

Derivation (Mallows CpC_pCp​)¶

Theorem 12.5: Mallows CpC_pCp​

Let a candidate model with ppp parameters have error sum of squares SSEp\mathrm{SSE}_pSSEp​, and let σ^2\hat{\sigma}^2σ^2 estimate σ2\sigma^2σ2 from the full model. Then

Cp=SSEpσ^2−(n−2p)C_p = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{\hat{\sigma}^2} - (n - 2p)Cp​=σ^2SSEp​​−(n−2p)

estimates the standardized total mean squared error Γp=σ−2∑iE{(Y^i−μi)2}\Gamma_p = \sigma^{-2}\sum_i E\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\}Γp​=σ−2∑i​E{(Y^i​−μi​)2} of the fitted values. A model with no bias has Cp≈pC_p \approx pCp​≈p; a model that omits an important predictor has CpC_pCp​ well above ppp.

Proof. We want a criterion that is small when a model’s fitted values Y^i\hat{Y}_iY^i​ are close to the true means μi=E{Yi}\mu_i = E\{Y_i\}μi​=E{Yi​}, across all nnn cases. Define the target

Γp=1σ2∑i=1nE{(Y^i−μi)2},\Gamma_p = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n E\bigl\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\bigr\},Γp​=σ21​i=1∑n​E{(Y^i​−μi​)2},

the total mean squared error of the fitted values, standardized by σ2\sigma^2σ2. Split each term into variance and squared bias with the identity E{W2}=Var⁡{W}+(E{W})2E\{W^2\} = \operatorname{Var}\{W\} + (E\{W\})^2E{W2}=Var{W}+(E{W})2 applied to W=Y^i−μiW = \hat{Y}_i - \mu_iW=Y^i​−μi​:

∑iE{(Y^i−μi)2}=∑iVar⁡{Y^i}⏟variance+∑i(E{Y^i}−μi)2⏟squared bias, call it B.\sum_i E\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\} = \underbrace{\sum_i \operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_i\}}_{\text{variance}} + \underbrace{\sum_i (E\{\hat{Y}_i\} - \mu_i)^2}_{\text{squared bias, call it } B}.i∑​E{(Y^i​−μi​)2}=variancei∑​Var{Y^i​}​​+squared bias, call it Bi∑​(E{Y^i​}−μi​)2​​.

For the variance term, the fitted values are Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY with the candidate model’s hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H, and ∑iVar⁡{Y^i}=σ2 tr(H)=pσ2\sum_i \operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_i\} = \sigma^2\,\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p\sigma^2∑i​Var{Y^i​}=σ2tr(H)=pσ2, using tr(H)=p\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = ptr(H)=p from 7.3 The hat matrix. So

∑iE{(Y^i−μi)2}=pσ2+B.\sum_i E\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\} = p\sigma^2 + B .i∑​E{(Y^i​−μi​)2}=pσ2+B.

Now connect BBB to something we can compute, the expected error sum of squares. Write Y=μ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=μ+ε and SSEp=∥(I−H)Y∥2\mathrm{SSE}_p = \|(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}\|^2SSEp​=∥(I−H)Y∥2. Then

E{SSEp}=∥(I−H)μ∥2+σ2 tr(I−H)=B+(n−p)σ2,E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} = \|(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\boldsymbol{\mu}\|^2 + \sigma^2\,\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}) = B + (n - p)\sigma^2,E{SSEp​}=∥(I−H)μ∥2+σ2tr(I−H)=B+(n−p)σ2,

because (I−H)μ(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\boldsymbol{\mu}(I−H)μ has iii-th entry μi−E{Y^i}\mu_i - E\{\hat{Y}_i\}μi​−E{Y^i​} so its squared length is BBB, and I−H\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}I−H is idempotent with trace n−pn - pn−p (again 7.3 The hat matrix). Solve for the bias: B=E{SSEp}−(n−p)σ2B = E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} - (n - p)\sigma^2B=E{SSEp​}−(n−p)σ2. Substitute into Γp\Gamma_pΓp​:

Γp=pσ2+Bσ2=pσ2+E{SSEp}−(n−p)σ2σ2=E{SSEp}σ2−(n−2p).\Gamma_p = \frac{p\sigma^2 + B}{\sigma^2} = \frac{p\sigma^2 + E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} - (n-p)\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} = \frac{E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\}}{\sigma^2} - (n - 2p) .Γp​=σ2pσ2+B​=σ2pσ2+E{SSEp​}−(n−p)σ2​=σ2E{SSEp​}​−(n−2p).

Replace the unknown E{SSEp}E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\}E{SSEp​} by the observed SSEp\mathrm{SSE}_pSSEp​ and the unknown σ2\sigma^2σ2 by the full model’s σ^2\hat{\sigma}^2σ^2, and you have Mallows’ statistic:

Cp=SSEpσ^2−(n−2p).C_p = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{\hat{\sigma}^2} - (n - 2p) .Cp​=σ^2SSEp​​−(n−2p).

Two consequences fall out. If the candidate model has no bias (B=0B = 0B=0, meaning it contains all the predictors that matter), then E{SSEp}=(n−p)σ2E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} = (n - p)\sigma^2E{SSEp​}=(n−p)σ2 and Γp=(n−p)−(n−2p)=p\Gamma_p = (n - p) - (n - 2p) = pΓp​=(n−p)−(n−2p)=p. So unbiased models have Cp≈pC_p \approx pCp​≈p: you look for models where CpC_pCp​ is both small and close to ppp. A model that omits an important predictor has B>0B > 0B>0, which pushes CpC_pCp​ well above ppp. ■\blacksquare■

R and Python¶

R’s leaps package searches all 213 subsets and reports the best model of each size.

Example 12.3: Best subsets and what the criteria choose

Question. Across all subset sizes, which models are best, and how many predictors do the criteria want?

Intuition. For each size, find the subset with the smallest SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, then read off CpC_pCp​, adjusted R2R^2R2, and BIC. The size where each criterion turns around is its choice.

Formula. The criteria above, evaluated at the best subset of each size.

Computation.

library(leaps)
rs <- regsubsets(brozek ~ age + weight + height + neck + chest + abdom + hip +
                   thigh + knee + ankle + biceps + forearm + wrist,
                 data = fat, nvmax = 13)
rss <- summary(rs)
rbind(Cp = round(rss$cp, 2), adjR2 = round(rss$adjr2, 4), BIC = round(rss$bic, 1))
           [,1]      [,2]      [,3]      [,4]      [,5]      [,6]      [,7]
Cp      72.3600   20.6800   14.3100    9.1500    8.4100    7.5700    6.2800
adjR2    0.6608    0.7165    0.7243    0.7308    0.7327    0.7347    0.7371
BIC   -262.4000 -303.1000 -305.6000 -307.1000 -304.4000 -301.7000 -299.6000
           [,8]      [,9]    [,10]     [,11]     [,12]     [,13]
Cp       6.1900    7.2500    8.550   10.0700   12.0000   14.0000
adjR2    0.7383    0.7383    0.738    0.7374    0.7364    0.7353
BIC   -296.2000 -291.7000 -286.900 -281.9000 -276.4000 -270.9000
c(min_Cp_size = which.min(rss$cp),
  max_adjR2_size = which.max(rss$adjr2),
  min_BIC_size = which.min(rss$bic))
names(which(rss$which[8, ]))
   min_Cp_size max_adjR2_size   min_BIC_size
             8              8              4
[1] "(Intercept)" "age"         "weight"      "neck"        "abdom"
[6] "hip"         "thigh"       "forearm"     "wrist"

Python has no leaps, so we run the same exhaustive search directly with itertools, finding the smallest-SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE subset of each size and its CpC_pCp​.

from itertools import combinations

X = fat[preds].to_numpy()
y = fat["brozek"].to_numpy()
n = len(fat)
Xf = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), X])
beta_f, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(Xf, y, rcond=None)
sigma2 = np.sum((y - Xf @ beta_f) ** 2) / (n - Xf.shape[1])

best_cp = {}
for k in range(1, 14):
    best_rss, best_set = np.inf, None
    for combo in combinations(range(13), k):
        Xs = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), X[:, combo]])
        b, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(Xs, y, rcond=None)
        rss = np.sum((y - Xs @ b) ** 2)
        if rss < best_rss:
            best_rss, best_set = rss, combo
    p = k + 1
    best_cp[k] = (round(best_rss / sigma2 - (n - 2 * p), 2),
                  [preds[i] for i in best_set])

for k, (cp, vars_) in best_cp.items():
    print(k, cp, vars_)
1 72.36 ['abdom']
2 20.68 ['weight', 'abdom']
3 14.31 ['weight', 'abdom', 'wrist']
4 9.15 ['weight', 'abdom', 'forearm', 'wrist']
5 8.41 ['weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'forearm', 'wrist']
6 7.57 ['age', 'weight', 'abdom', 'thigh', 'forearm', 'wrist']
7 6.28 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'thigh', 'forearm', 'wrist']
8 6.19 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'forearm', 'wrist']
9 7.25 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
10 8.55 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
11 10.07 ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
12 12.0 ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'chest', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
13 14.0 ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'chest', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'knee', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']

Interpretation. R and Python agree exactly on the best subset of each size and its CpC_pCp​. Mallows CpC_pCp​ and adjusted R2R^2R2 both bottom out (respectively, peak) at eight predictors: age, weight, neck, abdomen, hip, thigh, forearm, and wrist, with Cp=6.19C_p = 6.19Cp​=6.19. BIC, with its heavier penalty, prefers a leaner four-predictor model (weight, abdomen, forearm, wrist). The criteria agree that abdomen alone gets you most of the way (CpC_pCp​ drops from 72 to 21 once it is in) and that the last five predictors are deadweight, but they disagree on where exactly to stop. Figure 6 and Figure 7 show the two verdicts.

Deriving and checking PRESS¶

PRESS looks expensive: refitting the model nnn times, once per deleted case, is 252 fits per model here. A beautiful identity makes it free. You can get every deleted prediction from a single fit of the full data, using the ordinary residual and the leverage value.

Theorem 12.6: PRESS deleted-residual shortcut

For a least-squares fit with ordinary residual ei=Yi−Y^ie_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_iei​=Yi​−Y^i​ and leverage value hiih_{ii}hii​, the leave-one-out deleted residual is

Yi−Y^(i)=ei1−hii,soPRESS=∑i=1n(ei1−hii)2.Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}, \qquad\text{so}\qquad \mathrm{PRESS} = \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2 .Yi​−Y^(i)​=1−hii​ei​​,soPRESS=i=1∑n​(1−hii​ei​​)2.

Every leave-one-out prediction error then follows from a single fit of the full data.

Proof. Write the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X with iii-th row xi′\mathbf{x}_i'xi′​, the full-data estimate b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y, the fitted value Y^i=xi′b\hat{Y}_i = \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}Y^i​=xi′​b, the residual ei=Yi−Y^ie_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_iei​=Yi​−Y^i​, and the leverage value hii=xi′(X′X)−1xih_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_ihii​=xi′​(X′X)−1xi​ from 7.3 The hat matrix (the diagonal of the hat matrix). Let b(i)\mathbf{b}_{(i)}b(i)​ be the estimate from the data with case iii removed, and Y^(i)=xi′b(i)\hat{Y}_{(i)} = \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}_{(i)}Y^(i)​=xi′​b(i)​ the deleted prediction. We show

Yi−Y^(i)=ei1−hii.Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} .Yi​−Y^(i)​=1−hii​ei​​.

Deleting case iii changes the cross-product matrix and vector to X′X−xixi′\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i'X′X−xi​xi′​ and X′Y−xiYi\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{x}_i Y_iX′Y−xi​Yi​. The Sherman-Morrison inverse-update identity (derived and checked in 9.3 Influence: which points actually change the fit) gives

(X′X−xixi′)−1=(X′X)−1+(X′X)−1xixi′(X′X)−11−hii.(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i')^{-1} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \frac{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}}{1 - h_{ii}} .(X′X−xi​xi′​)−1=(X′X)−1+1−hii​(X′X)−1xi​xi′​(X′X)−1​.

Write u=(X′X)−1xi\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_iu=(X′X)−1xi​, so that xi′u=hii\mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{u} = h_{ii}xi′​u=hii​ and xi′(X′X)−1X′Y=xi′b=Y^i\mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b} = \hat{Y}_ixi′​(X′X)−1X′Y=xi′​b=Y^i​. Multiply the updated inverse by X′Y−xiYi\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{x}_i Y_iX′Y−xi​Yi​:

b(i)=b−uYi+u (Y^i−hiiYi)1−hii=b+u −Yi(1−hii)+Y^i−hiiYi1−hii=b−u ei1−hii,\mathbf{b}_{(i)} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{u}Y_i + \frac{\mathbf{u}\,(\hat{Y}_i - h_{ii} Y_i)}{1 - h_{ii}} = \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{u}\,\frac{-Y_i(1 - h_{ii}) + \hat{Y}_i - h_{ii}Y_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{u}\,\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} ,b(i)​=b−uYi​+1−hii​u(Y^i​−hii​Yi​)​=b+u1−hii​−Yi​(1−hii​)+Y^i​−hii​Yi​​=b−u1−hii​ei​​,

where the numerator collapsed to Y^i−Yi=−ei\hat{Y}_i - Y_i = -e_iY^i​−Yi​=−ei​. Now form the deleted prediction:

Yi−Y^(i)=Yi−xi′b(i)=(Yi−xi′b)+xi′u ei1−hii=ei+hii ei1−hii=ei1−hii.Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}_{(i)} = (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}) + \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{u}\,\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = e_i + \frac{h_{ii}\,e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} .Yi​−Y^(i)​=Yi​−xi′​b(i)​=(Yi​−xi′​b)+xi′​u1−hii​ei​​=ei​+1−hii​hii​ei​​=1−hii​ei​​.

So each deleted residual is just the ordinary residual divided by 1−hii1 - h_{ii}1−hii​, and

PRESS=∑i=1n(ei1−hii)2.\mathrm{PRESS} = \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2 .PRESS=i=1∑n​(1−hii​ei​​)2.

High-leverage points (large hiih_{ii}hii​) get their residuals magnified, which is right: those are the cases the model props itself up on, and it predicts them worst when they are removed. ■\blacksquare■

Example 12.4: Selection criteria and PRESS for a candidate model

Question. For the eight-predictor model CpC_pCp​ chose, compute CpC_pCp​ by hand, compare AIC and BIC to the full model, and confirm the PRESS shortcut against brute force.

Intuition. CpC_pCp​ needs only SSEp\mathrm{SSE}_pSSEp​ and the full-model σ^2\hat{\sigma}^2σ^2. PRESS needs only the residuals and leverages of one fit; brute force refits 252 times and must agree.

Formula. Cp=SSEp/σ^2−(n−2p)C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p)Cp​=SSEp​/σ^2−(n−2p), and PRESS=∑(ei/(1−hii))2\mathrm{PRESS} = \sum (e_i/(1 - h_{ii}))^2PRESS=∑(ei​/(1−hii​))2.

Computation.

cand <- lm(brozek ~ age + weight + neck + abdom + hip + thigh + forearm + wrist,
           data = fat)
n <- nrow(fat)
sigma2 <- summary(full)$sigma^2       # full-model MSE, the estimate of sigma^2
p_cand <- length(coef(cand))          # parameters in the candidate (incl. intercept)
SSEp <- sum(resid(cand)^2)
Cp <- SSEp / sigma2 - (n - 2 * p_cand)
c(p = p_cand, SSEp = round(SSEp, 2), Cp = round(Cp, 3))
       p     SSEp       Cp
   9.000 3819.990    6.191
c(full_AIC = AIC(full), cand_AIC = AIC(cand),
  full_BIC = BIC(full), cand_BIC = BIC(cand))
full_AIC cand_AIC full_BIC cand_BIC
1427.916 1420.225 1480.857 1455.520
press <- function(model) {
  h <- hatvalues(model)
  sum((resid(model) / (1 - h))^2)
}
c(PRESS_full = press(full), PRESS_cand = press(cand))
PRESS_full PRESS_cand
  4306.122   4139.682
press_bruteforce <- function(form, data) {
  total <- 0
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(data))) {
    fit_i <- lm(form, data = data[-i, ])
    pred_i <- as.numeric(predict(fit_i, newdata = data[i, ]))
    total <- total + (data$brozek[i] - pred_i)^2
  }
  total
}
candform <- brozek ~ age + weight + neck + abdom + hip + thigh + forearm + wrist
c(shortcut = press(cand),
  bruteforce = press_bruteforce(candform, fat))
  shortcut bruteforce
  4139.682   4139.682

Now Python. Note the different AIC and BIC constants, as promised: only within-language differences matter.

cand_vars = ["age", "weight", "neck", "abdom", "hip", "thigh", "forearm", "wrist"]
cand = smf.ols("brozek ~ " + " + ".join(cand_vars), data=fat).fit()
p_cand = len(cand.params)
SSEp = np.sum(cand.resid ** 2)
Cp = SSEp / full.scale - (n - 2 * p_cand)
print(p_cand, round(SSEp, 2), round(Cp, 3))
9 3819.99 6.191
def press(model):
    h = model.get_influence().hat_matrix_diag
    return np.sum((model.resid / (1 - h)) ** 2)

print(round(press(full), 2), round(press(cand), 2))
4306.12 4139.68
def press_bruteforce(formula, data):
    total = 0.0
    for i in range(len(data)):
        fit_i = smf.ols(formula, data=data.drop(data.index[i])).fit()
        pred_i = fit_i.predict(data.iloc[[i]]).iloc[0]
        total += (data["brozek"].iloc[i] - pred_i) ** 2
    return total

candform = "brozek ~ " + " + ".join(cand_vars)
print(round(press(cand), 2), round(press_bruteforce(candform, fat), 2))
4139.68 4139.68

Interpretation. The candidate’s Cp=6.19C_p = 6.19Cp​=6.19 against p=9p = 9p=9 parameters sits right on the Cp≈pC_p \approx pCp​≈p target, the mark of an unbiased model, and it matches leaps exactly. Its AIC is lower than the full model’s in both languages (1420 versus 1428 in R), so AIC also prefers the smaller model. Best of all, its PRESS (4140) is lower than the full model’s (4306): the eight-predictor model predicts left-out men better than the thirteen-predictor model, direct evidence that the extra five predictors were hurting, not helping. And the one-line shortcut reproduces the brute-force PRESS to the digit (4139.682), so we never have to refit 252 times again.

Durable skill: Score models by prediction, not by fit

Any model can be made to fit the data you already have; the question is whether it will predict the data you do not. Every criterion in this section is a different attempt to answer that question honestly, by charging for complexity (CpC_pCp​, AIC, BIC, adjusted R2R^2R2) or by predicting held-out cases (PRESS). When someone shows you a model with a dazzling R2R^2R2, ask what it scores on data it has not seen. This one reflex separates real predictive work from curve-fitting, in statistics and in machine learning alike.

Try it 12.2

A four-predictor model has SSEp=4000\mathrm{SSE}_p = 4000SSEp​=4000, and the full-model estimate is σ^2=15.9\hat{\sigma}^2 = 15.9σ^2=15.9 with n=252n = 252n=252. Compute CpC_pCp​ (here p=5p = 5p=5). Is this model’s CpC_pCp​ close to ppp, and what would it mean if CpC_pCp​ were much larger than ppp?

Solution

Cp=SSEp/σ^2−(n−2p)=4000/15.9−(252−10)=251.6−242=9.6C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p) = 4000/15.9 - (252 - 10) = 251.6 - 242 = 9.6Cp​=SSEp​/σ^2−(n−2p)=4000/15.9−(252−10)=251.6−242=9.6. That is somewhat above p=5p = 5p=5, suggesting a little bias: this small model may be leaving out a useful predictor. A CpC_pCp​ much larger than ppp signals real bias, meaning the model omits predictors that matter and its predictions are systematically off. A CpC_pCp​ near ppp (like the eight-predictor model’s 6.19 against 9) signals a model with negligible bias.

12.3 The dangers of automatic selection¶

Intuition¶

It is tempting to automate the search: let the computer add and drop predictors by their ppp-values until nothing improves. This is stepwise selection (Definition 12.7), and it is available in one line in every statistics package. It is also the source of more bad science than almost any other routine procedure, and you should understand exactly why before you ever use it.

Definition 12.7: Stepwise selection

Stepwise selection is an automatic search (forward, backward, or both directions) that repeatedly adds or drops predictors by their ppp-values until no single change improves the model. It is prone to overfitting and produces invalid post-selection inference.

Two things go wrong. The first is overfitting (Definition 12.8): a procedure that chases the best fit on your sample will seize on accidents of that sample, patterns that are noise and will not repeat. The second, subtler and worse, is post-selection inference (Definition 12.9): once you have chosen a model because it fit well, the ppp-values, confidence intervals, and FFF tests that software prints for that model are wrong. They were derived assuming the model was fixed in advance, before you looked at the data. Choosing the model by looking at the data breaks that assumption, and the reported significance becomes fiction.

Definition 12.8: Overfitting

Overfitting is fitting the noise in the training sample: the model chases accidents of the data at hand, so it fits that sample well but predicts new data poorly.

Definition 12.9: Post-selection inference

Post-selection inference is inference (ppp-values, confidence intervals, FFF tests) reported for a model chosen from the same data. It is invalid because the standard distributions assume the model was fixed before the data were seen, which selection violates.

A demonstration with pure noise¶

The cleanest way to see the disaster is to run selection on data with no signal at all. Take a response and forty predictors that are all independent random noise, unrelated to the response and to each other. Honest testing should almost never find anything. Selection finds plenty.

set.seed(4210)
noise <- data.frame(y = rnorm(100), matrix(rnorm(100 * 40), 100, 40))
names(noise)[-1] <- paste0("x", 1:40)
pvals <- sapply(1:40, function(j)
  summary(lm(noise$y ~ noise[[paste0("x", j)]]))$coefficients[2, 4])
best5 <- order(pvals)[1:5]
selected <- lm(y ~ ., data = noise[, c("y", paste0("x", best5))])
fstat <- summary(selected)$fstatistic
c(R2 = summary(selected)$r.squared,
  overall_F_pvalue = as.numeric(pf(fstat[1], fstat[2], fstat[3], lower.tail = FALSE)))
              R2 overall_F_pvalue
      0.13497399       0.01663716

We picked the five predictors with the smallest individual ppp-values out of forty pure-noise columns, fit a model on just those five, and got an overall FFF-test ppp-value of 0.017. By the usual rule, that model is “significant at the 0.05 level,” and its R2=0.135R^2 = 0.135R2=0.135 looks respectable. Every bit of it is an illusion: there is no relationship in the data at all. The selection step manufactured the significance by cherry-picking, and the FFF test, blind to the cherry-picking, reported it as real.

This is not a one-off fluke of the seed. Figure 8 repeats the whole experiment 2000 times. Under honest testing the overall FFF ppp-value should be uniform on [0,1][0, 1][0,1], so only 5 percent should fall below 0.05. After selection, 94 percent do.

What to do instead¶

None of this means variable selection is forbidden. It means three things. First, prefer the criterion-based methods of Section 12.2 (compare whole models by CpC_pCp​, BIC, or cross-validated error) over ppp-value-driven stepwise search, and report that you searched. Second, never quote the ppp-values from a selected model as if the model had been fixed in advance; recall from 8.4 The general linear test that the FFF test’s distribution assumes the comparison was chosen before seeing the data. Third, and most reliably, judge the final model on data it was not chosen with. That is validation, the subject of the next section, and it is the only honest referee for a model you built by searching.

Try it 12.3

A classmate runs backward stepwise selection, arrives at a five-predictor model, and reports that “all five predictors are significant at p<0.01p < 0.01p<0.01, so the model is excellent.” What is wrong with this claim, and what evidence would actually support the model?

Solution

The ppp-values are contaminated by the selection: the predictors were kept because they had small ppp-values, so of course the survivors look significant. Those ppp-values no longer have their advertised meaning, and “all five significant” is nearly guaranteed by the procedure, not by a real effect (the noise demonstration got a significant model from nothing at all). Honest support would come from out-of-sample evidence: a low cross-validated error, or good prediction on a held-out test set that played no part in choosing the model.

12.4 Validation: train, test, and cross-validate¶

Intuition¶

The residuals from a fitted model tell you how well it fits the data it was built on, which is always flattering. To know how well a model predicts, you must test it on data it has never seen. The idea echoes the out-of-sample thinking behind the bootstrap in 5.4 The bootstrap for regression and the prediction intervals of 3.5 Prediction interval for a new observation: honest error estimates come from treating some data as genuinely new.

The simplest version splits the data once: a training set (Definition 12.10) to fit the model, and a test set, held back, to measure its error. The split must be made before any modeling, and the test set must never influence a single choice. A more efficient version, kkk-fold cross-validation (Definition 12.11), splits the data into kkk equal folds, then rotates: each fold takes a turn as the test set while the other k−1k - 1k−1 folds train the model. Averaging the kkk test errors uses every case for testing exactly once, so it wastes no data. Figure 9 shows the rotation.

Definition 12.10: Training and test sets

A training set is the part of the data used to fit a model; a test set is a held-back part used only to measure the fitted model’s prediction error. The split is made before any modeling, and the test set influences no modeling choice.

Definition 12.11: kkk-fold cross-validation

kkk-fold cross-validation splits the data into kkk equal folds and rotates, letting each fold serve once as the test set while the other k−1k-1k−1 folds train the model. Averaging the kkk test errors tests every case exactly once, wasting no data.

We measure error with the root mean squared error (Definition 12.12), the typical size of a prediction miss, in the units of the response (percentage points of body fat). A smaller one means the model is closer, on average, to the truth for a new man.

Definition 12.12: Root mean squared error

The root mean squared error over mmm held-out cases is

RMSE=1m∑i=1m(Yi−Y^i)2,\mathrm{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2},RMSE=m1​i=1∑m​(Yi​−Y^i​)2​,

the typical size of a prediction miss, expressed in the units of the response.

The single train/test split¶

Split the 252 men with a fixed seed so the analysis reproduces, fit on the training set, and compare training error to test error.

Example 12.5: A seeded train/test split

Question. Fit the full and candidate models on a training set. How do their training and test errors compare?

Intuition. Training error rewards complexity; test error is the honest measure. If the full model overfits, its test error will be worse than the smaller model’s despite a better training fit.

Formula. RMSE\mathrm{RMSE}RMSE on the held-out test set.

Computation.

set.seed(4210)
train_idx <- sort(sample(seq_len(n), size = 189))
train <- fat[train_idx, ]
test <- fat[-train_idx, ]
c(n_train = nrow(train), n_test = nrow(test))
n_train  n_test
    189      63
rmse <- function(model, newdata) {
  pred <- predict(model, newdata = newdata)
  sqrt(mean((newdata$brozek - pred)^2))
}
full_tr <- lm(formula(full), data = train)
cand_tr <- lm(candform, data = train)
rbind(full = c(train = rmse(full_tr, train), test = rmse(full_tr, test)),
      cand = c(train = rmse(cand_tr, train), test = rmse(cand_tr, test)))
        train     test
full 3.894608 4.099456
cand 3.948575 3.892048
rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
perm = rng.permutation(n)
test_idx = np.sort(perm[:63])
train_idx = np.sort(perm[63:])
train = fat.iloc[train_idx]
test = fat.iloc[test_idx]
print(len(train), len(test))
189 63
def rmse(model, data):
    pred = model.predict(data)
    return np.sqrt(np.mean((data["brozek"] - pred) ** 2))

full_tr = smf.ols("brozek ~ " + " + ".join(preds), data=train).fit()
cand_tr = smf.ols(candform, data=train).fit()
print("full", round(rmse(full_tr, train), 3), round(rmse(full_tr, test), 3))
print("cand", round(rmse(cand_tr, train), 3), round(rmse(cand_tr, test), 3))
full 3.861 4.035
cand 3.903 3.929

Interpretation. The full model fits the training data slightly better than the candidate (training RMSE 3.89 versus 3.95 in R), exactly as expected: more predictors always fit the sample better. But on the held-out test set the order reverses. The candidate predicts better (test RMSE 3.89 versus 4.10 in R; 3.93 versus 4.04 in Python). The five extra predictors were fitting training-set noise, and that noise did not carry over. R and Python use different random splits, so their exact numbers differ, but both tell the same story: the smaller model generalizes as well or better.

kkk-fold cross-validation, written by hand¶

A single split is noisy: a lucky or unlucky test set can mislead. Cross-validation averages over kkk splits. It is worth writing the loop yourself once, so the mechanism holds no mystery. Assign each case a fold number, then loop: train on the other folds, predict the held-out fold, collect the squared errors.

Example 12.6: Ten-fold cross-validation by hand

Question. Compare the full and candidate models by 10-fold cross-validated RMSE on the whole dataset.

Intuition. Rotate through ten folds, predicting each once. The averaged error is a stable estimate of prediction accuracy, and it should again favor the smaller model.

Formula. CV RMSE=1K∑k=1KMSEk\mathrm{CV\ RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathrm{MSE}_k}CV RMSE=K1​∑k=1K​MSEk​​, where MSEk\mathrm{MSE}_kMSEk​ is the mean squared error on fold kkk.

Computation.

set.seed(4210)
K <- 10
folds <- sample(rep(1:K, length.out = n))
cv_rmse <- function(form) {
  sq_err <- numeric(K)
  for (k in 1:K) {
    fit_k <- lm(form, data = fat[folds != k, ])
    pred_k <- predict(fit_k, newdata = fat[folds == k, ])
    sq_err[k] <- mean((fat$brozek[folds == k] - pred_k)^2)
  }
  sqrt(mean(sq_err))
}
c(full_CV = cv_rmse(formula(full)), cand_CV = cv_rmse(candform))
 full_CV  cand_CV
4.130328 4.042110
rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
folds = rng.integers(0, 10, size=n)
def cv_rmse(formula):
    sq_err = []
    for k in range(10):
        fit_k = smf.ols(formula, data=fat[folds != k]).fit()
        pred_k = fit_k.predict(fat[folds == k])
        sq_err.append(np.mean((fat["brozek"][folds == k] - pred_k) ** 2))
    return np.sqrt(np.mean(sq_err))

print(round(cv_rmse("brozek ~ " + " + ".join(preds)), 3),
      round(cv_rmse(candform), 3))
4.157 4.046

Interpretation. The candidate’s cross-validated RMSE (4.04 in R, 4.05 in Python) beats the full model’s (4.13 in R, 4.16 in Python) by about 0.1 percentage points of body fat. The gap is modest but consistent across languages and across the single-split result, and it points the same way every time: the eight-predictor model predicts new men better than the thirteen-predictor model. Figure 10 traces this over every model size, and it is the central picture of the chapter.

Durable skill: Hold out data before you fall in love with a model

The single most reliable guardrail against fooling yourself is to set aside data you will not touch until the very end, and to judge the final model only on that. It costs you nothing but discipline, and it catches overfitting, data leakage, and post-selection optimism all at once. Every serious predictive project, from a course paper to a production machine-learning system, lives or dies on whether its held-out evaluation was truly held out. Build the habit now: decide your test set first, and do not peek.

Try it 12.4

Explain why training RMSE always decreases (or stays equal) as you add predictors, but cross-validated RMSE can increase. Which one estimates how the model will do on a new man, and why?

Solution

Adding a predictor gives least squares more freedom, so it can only reduce (never increase) the sum of squared residuals on the training data; training RMSE therefore falls monotonically. But a predictor that only fits training-set noise will make predictions on held-out data worse, so cross-validated RMSE can rise once you pass the useful predictors. Cross-validated RMSE estimates performance on a new man, because it always scores predictions on cases that did not train the model. Training RMSE cannot, because the model has already seen those cases.

12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso¶

Intuition¶

Selection is a blunt instrument: a predictor is either in (full coefficient) or out (zero). Shrinkage (Definition 12.13) offers a gentler alternative. Instead of deleting predictors, it keeps them all but pulls their coefficients toward zero, trading a little bias for a large drop in variance. When predictors are collinear, ordinary least squares can produce enormous coefficients that cancel each other (the negative weight from Example 12.1). Shrinkage refuses to let coefficients grow that large, which stabilizes them.

Definition 12.13: Shrinkage

Shrinkage pulls a model’s coefficients toward zero by adding a penalty on their size, trading a little bias for a large drop in variance. It keeps every predictor rather than deleting any.

Key idea

The bias-variance trade-off is the single idea under every method in this chapter. A flexible model (many predictors, no shrinkage) has low bias but high variance; a rigid one (few predictors, heavy shrinkage) has high bias but low variance. Prediction error is their sum plus irreducible noise, so the best predictor lives in between, as Figure 11 shows. Selection and shrinkage are both ways of aiming for that sweet spot.

Formula¶

Ordinary least squares minimizes the residual sum of squares. Ridge (Definition 12.14) and lasso (Definition 12.15) add a penalty on the size of the coefficients.

Definition 12.14: Ridge regression

Ridge regression minimizes least squares plus an L2L_2L2​ penalty on the slopes:

β^ridge=arg⁡min⁡β∑i=1n(Yi−xi′β)2+λ∑k=1qβk2.\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{ridge}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})^2 + \lambda \sum_{k=1}^{q} \beta_k^2 .β^​ridge​=argβmin​i=1∑n​(Yi​−xi′​β)2+λk=1∑q​βk2​.

It shrinks every coefficient toward zero but rarely to exactly zero.

Definition 12.15: Lasso

The lasso minimizes least squares plus an L1L_1L1​ penalty on the slopes:

β^lasso=arg⁡min⁡β∑i=1n(Yi−xi′β)2+λ∑k=1q∣βk∣.\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{lasso}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})^2 + \lambda \sum_{k=1}^{q} |\beta_k| .β^​lasso​=argβmin​i=1∑n​(Yi​−xi′​β)2+λk=1∑q​∣βk​∣.

Because its penalty has corners on the axes, it can set coefficients exactly to zero, doing variable selection and shrinkage at once.

  • The first term is the usual least-squares loss; it wants a good fit.

  • The second term is the penalty; it wants small coefficients. The sum runs over the qqq slope coefficients (not the intercept), and predictors are standardized first so the penalty treats them fairly.

  • λ≥0\lambda \ge 0λ≥0 is the tuning parameter that sets the strength of the penalty. At λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0 both reduce to least squares; as λ→∞\lambda \to \inftyλ→∞ all coefficients are crushed to zero. We choose λ\lambdaλ by cross-validation.

Ridge uses the squared penalty (∑βk2\sum \beta_k^2∑βk2​, the L2L_2L2​ norm); lasso uses the absolute-value penalty (∑∣βk∣\sum |\beta_k|∑∣βk​∣, the L1L_1L1​ norm). The difference sounds minor and is not. Ridge shrinks every coefficient toward zero but rarely to exactly zero. Lasso can set coefficients exactly to zero, so it does variable selection and shrinkage at once. The reason is geometric, shown in Figure 12: the lasso’s constraint region is a diamond with corners on the axes, and the best-fitting contour tends to touch a corner, where a coefficient is zero. Ridge’s constraint region is a smooth disk with no corners.

R and Python¶

The R package glmnet is the standard tool. Its cv.glmnet fits the whole path of λ\lambdaλ values and picks one by cross-validation, returning lambda.min (the error minimizer) and lambda.1se (the largest λ\lambdaλ within one standard error of the minimum, a deliberately sparser choice). Predictors are standardized automatically.

Example 12.7: Ridge and lasso on the body-fat data

Question. Fit lasso and ridge to the training set, see which predictors the lasso keeps, and compare test-set prediction to ordinary least squares.

Intuition. Cross-validation picks the penalty. The lasso should zero out the redundant predictors; both should predict comparably to a well-chosen OLS model.

Formula. The ridge and lasso objectives above, with λ\lambdaλ chosen by cross-validation.

Computation.

library(glmnet)
Xtr <- as.matrix(train[, preds]); Ytr <- train$brozek
Xte <- as.matrix(test[, preds]);  Yte <- test$brozek
set.seed(4210)
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(Xtr, Ytr, alpha = 1)
c(lambda_min = cv_lasso$lambda.min, lambda_1se = cv_lasso$lambda.1se)
lambda_min lambda_1se
 0.1876241  0.9123410
round(as.vector(coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.1se")), 4)
 [1] -27.8141   0.0000   0.0000  -0.0415   0.0000   0.0000   0.5362   0.0000
 [9]   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000
set.seed(4210)
cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(Xtr, Ytr, alpha = 0)
pred_lasso <- predict(cv_lasso, newx = Xte, s = "lambda.min")
pred_ridge <- predict(cv_ridge, newx = Xte, s = "lambda.min")
c(lasso_test_RMSE = sqrt(mean((Yte - pred_lasso)^2)),
  ridge_test_RMSE = sqrt(mean((Yte - pred_ridge)^2)),
  ols_full_test_RMSE = rmse(full_tr, test))
   lasso_test_RMSE    ridge_test_RMSE ols_full_test_RMSE
          4.344938           4.539166           4.099456

Python uses scikit-learn’s LassoCV and RidgeCV. We standardize the predictors by hand first, as glmnet does internally.

from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV, RidgeCV
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

scaler = StandardScaler().fit(train[preds])
Xtr = scaler.transform(train[preds]); ytr = train["brozek"].to_numpy()
Xte = scaler.transform(test[preds]);  yte = test["brozek"].to_numpy()
lasso = LassoCV(cv=10, random_state=4210).fit(Xtr, ytr)
ridge = RidgeCV(alphas=np.logspace(-2, 3, 100)).fit(Xtr, ytr)
lasso_rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((yte - lasso.predict(Xte)) ** 2))
ridge_rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((yte - ridge.predict(Xte)) ** 2))
print("lasso CV nonzero:", int(np.sum(lasso.coef_ != 0)), "of 13")
print("lasso test RMSE:", round(lasso_rmse, 3),
      " ridge test RMSE:", round(ridge_rmse, 3))
lasso CV nonzero: 13 of 13
lasso test RMSE: 4.019  ridge test RMSE: 4.019
from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso

for a in [0.1, 0.5, 1.0]:
    fit_a = Lasso(alpha=a).fit(Xtr, ytr)
    kept = [p for p, c in zip(preds, fit_a.coef_) if c != 0]
    print(f"alpha={a}: {len(kept)} kept -> {kept}")
alpha=0.1: 11 kept -> ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
alpha=0.5: 4 kept -> ['age', 'height', 'abdom', 'wrist']
alpha=1.0: 2 kept -> ['height', 'abdom']

Interpretation. The lasso does exactly what it promises. At the sparser lambda.1se, R’s glmnet keeps only two predictors with nonzero coefficients, abdomen (0.54) and height (-0.04), zeroing the other eleven; the intercept (-27.8) is not penalized. Python shows the same behavior as the penalty grows: at a light penalty eleven predictors survive, at a heavier one only two (height and abdomen). Figure 13 draws the whole path. On this particular split the shrinkage models predict slightly worse than the eight-predictor OLS model (test RMSE around 4.3 to 4.5 versus 4.1). That is honest and worth stating plainly. With n=189n = 189n=189 men and only thirteen predictors, ordinary least squares is not badly overfit, so shrinkage has little variance to save here. Its payoff shows up when predictors vastly outnumber cases, or when collinearity is so severe that OLS coefficients become unusable. What the lasso always gives you, even here, is a compact, automatically selected model you can read.

The plain summary of shrinkage¶

Ridge and lasso answer the collinearity and selection problems in one move. Ridge keeps every predictor but shrinks the coefficients so no two collinear predictors can blow up against each other; it is the natural tool when you believe many predictors each contribute a little. Lasso shrinks and selects at once, zeroing the coefficients it does not need, so it is the natural tool when you believe only a few predictors matter and you want the model to find them. Both replace the discrete, unstable question “which predictors are in?” with a smooth, tunable dial λ\lambdaλ, set by cross-validation. That is why shrinkage, not stepwise selection, is the modern default when prediction is the goal.

Try it 12.5

As λ\lambdaλ increases from 0 toward infinity in ridge regression, describe what happens to the coefficients, to the model’s variance, and to its bias. What does λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0 give, and what does a very large λ\lambdaλ give?

Solution

At λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0 the penalty vanishes and ridge is ordinary least squares: lowest bias, highest variance. As λ\lambdaλ grows, the coefficients shrink toward zero, the model’s variance falls (the estimates stabilize, less sensitive to the sample), and its bias rises (the shrunk coefficients are systematically too small in magnitude). As λ→∞\lambda \to \inftyλ→∞ all slope coefficients go to zero and the model predicts the mean response for everyone: zero variance, maximum bias. Cross-validation chooses the λ\lambdaλ that balances the two for the smallest prediction error.

12.6 Chapter summary¶

You can now handle a regression with more predictors than you can trust. You diagnosed multicollinearity with the variance inflation factor and derived it from an auxiliary regression; you compared candidate models with adjusted R2R^2R2, Mallows CpC_pCp​, AIC, BIC, and PRESS, deriving CpC_pCp​ and proving the deleted-residual shortcut that makes leave-one-out free; you saw, in a pure-noise demonstration, why stepwise selection overfits and why post-selection ppp-values lie; you wrote a kkk-fold cross-validation loop by hand and used a held-out test set to judge a model honestly; and you described and ran ridge and lasso, reading shrinkage as a bias-variance trade made by a tunable penalty. On the body-fat data the eight-predictor candidate beat the full model on every honest measure: lower CpC_pCp​, lower PRESS, and lower cross-validated error.

Figure 14 puts the whole chapter on one page: diagnose the redundancy, let your goal decide the remedy, choose a model by a prediction-minded score, and confirm the winner on data it never saw.

Key results at a glance

ResultStatement or formulaValid when
Coefficient variance (Theorem 12.4)Var⁡{bk}=σ2Skk(1−Rk2)=σ2Skk VIFk\operatorname{Var}\{b_k\} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}(1 - R_k^2)} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{S_{kk}}\,\mathrm{VIF}_kVar{bk​}=Skk​(1−Rk2​)σ2​=Skk​σ2​VIFk​general linear model, uncorrelated equal-variance errors
Variance inflation factor (Definition 12.2)VIFk=1/(1−Rk2)\mathrm{VIF}_k = 1/(1 - R_k^2)VIFk​=1/(1−Rk2​), with Rk2R_k^2Rk2​ the auxiliary R2R^2R2any multiple regression
Adjusted R2R^2R21−SSEp/(n−p)SSTO/(n−1)1 - \dfrac{\mathrm{SSE}_p/(n-p)}{\mathrm{SSTO}/(n-1)}1−SSTO/(n−1)SSEp​/(n−p)​; bigger is bettercomparing models of different size
Mallows CpC_pCp​ (Theorem 12.5)Cp=SSEp/σ^2−(n−2p)C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p)Cp​=SSEp​/σ^2−(n−2p); unbiased model has Cp≈pC_p \approx pCp​≈pσ^2\hat{\sigma}^2σ^2 from a near-unbiased full model
AIC / BICnln⁡(SSEp/n)+{2p, pln⁡n}n\ln(\mathrm{SSE}_p/n) + \{2p,\ p\ln n\}nln(SSEp​/n)+{2p, plnn}; smaller is bettercompared within one program
PRESS shortcut (Theorem 12.6)PRESS=∑(ei/(1−hii))2\mathrm{PRESS} = \sum (e_i/(1 - h_{ii}))^2PRESS=∑(ei​/(1−hii​))2least-squares linear fit
CV RMSE1K∑kMSEk\sqrt{\frac{1}{K}\sum_k \mathrm{MSE}_k}K1​∑k​MSEk​​; smaller is betterfolds disjoint, tested once each
Ridge / lasso$\min \mathrm{RSS} + \lambda\sum {\beta_k^2,\\beta_k

Key terms. Multicollinearity, variance inflation factor, auxiliary regression, adjusted R2R^2R2, Mallows CpC_pCp​, AIC, BIC, PRESS, deleted residual, stepwise selection, overfitting, post-selection inference, training and test sets, kkk-fold cross-validation, root mean squared error, bias-variance trade-off, shrinkage, ridge regression, lasso, tuning parameter λ\lambdaλ.

You should now be able to

  • Diagnose multicollinearity with the VIF and derive it from an auxiliary regression (Theorem 12.4).

  • Explain what multicollinearity does and does not harm, and list the standard remedies.

  • Compute and compare adjusted R2R^2R2, Mallows CpC_pCp​, AIC, BIC, and PRESS.

  • Derive Mallows CpC_pCp​ (Theorem 12.5) and prove the PRESS deleted-residual shortcut (Theorem 12.6).

  • Explain, bluntly, why stepwise selection and post-selection inference mislead.

  • Implement kkk-fold cross-validation by hand in R and Python.

  • Describe ridge and lasso shrinkage and demonstrate them on the body-fat data.

Where this fits. This chapter is the FIT and CHECK stages of The modeling workflow done together and repeatedly: you fit many models, check each for collinearity and prediction error, and iterate toward one you can defend. Chapters 8 through 11 gave you a single multiple regression and the tools to interpret and diagnose it; this chapter faced the harder situation of many competing models and taught you to choose among them by prediction rather than fit. The validation mindset carries straight into 13. Logistic regression, where the same held-out thinking becomes the classification metrics (accuracy, ROC) that judge a logistic model, and the shrinkage and criterion ideas reappear wherever a model has more parameters than the data can comfortably support.

12.7 Frequently asked questions¶

Q1. Does multicollinearity bias my coefficients? No. Least squares stays unbiased under collinearity: on average the coefficients are still correct. What collinearity does is inflate their variance, so any single sample’s estimates can be wildly off, with flipped signs and huge standard errors. The average is fine; the individual estimate is unreliable.

Q2. If collinearity does not hurt prediction, why care about it? It depends on your goal. If you only want to predict body fat for men like those in the data, a collinear model predicts fine and you can ignore the VIFs. If you want to interpret coefficients (to say which measurement matters and by how much), collinearity makes those coefficients meaningless, and you must fix it by dropping, combining, or shrinking predictors.

Q3. Which selection criterion should I use? For interpretation, BIC and its heavier penalty give you a lean, defensible model. For prediction, cross-validated error is the most direct answer, and Mallows CpC_pCp​ is a fast, well-motivated stand-in. Do not agonize over small differences: report that the criteria pointed to a range of models (here, four to eight predictors) and pick within that range for a reason you can state.

Q4. Why is the CpC_pCp​ of the full model always equal to the number of its parameters? By construction. Cp=SSEp/σ^2−(n−2p)C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p)Cp​=SSEp​/σ^2−(n−2p), and for the full model σ^2=SSEfull/(n−pfull)\hat{\sigma}^2 = \mathrm{SSE}_{\text{full}}/(n - p_{\text{full}})σ^2=SSEfull​/(n−pfull​), so SSEfull/σ^2=n−pfull\mathrm{SSE}_{\text{full}}/\hat{\sigma}^2 = n - p_{\text{full}}SSEfull​/σ^2=n−pfull​ and $C_p = (n - p_{\text{full}})

  • (n - 2p_{\text{full}}) = p_{\text{full}}.Thefullmodelisthereferencepoint,soits. The full model is the reference point, so its.Thefullmodelisthereferencepoint,soitsC_plandsexactlyonthe lands exactly on the landsexactlyontheC_p = p$ line.

Q5. Is a lower PRESS always better? As a comparison of models on the same data, yes: lower PRESS means better leave-one-out prediction. But PRESS is still an in-sample estimate in the sense that it uses the same 252 men to build and to test (just never the same man for both at once), so it can be optimistic if the whole dataset is unusual. A truly held-out test set, or kkk-fold cross-validation repeated with different seeds, is a stronger check.

Q6. Can I just trust the model my software’s step() function returns? No. Stepwise selection overfits and its reported ppp-values are invalid (Section 12.3). If you use it, treat its output as a candidate to be validated on held-out data, never as a finished model, and never quote its coefficients’ significance as if the model had been chosen in advance.

Q7. Lasso and stepwise both drop predictors. What is the difference? Stepwise makes hard, unstable in-or-out decisions by ppp-value and reports dishonest inference. Lasso makes the decision continuously through a single penalty λ\lambdaλ chosen by cross-validation, shrinking coefficients smoothly and turning them off only as the penalty grows. Lasso is more stable, has a coherent bias-variance justification, and is built for prediction. When in doubt, prefer it.

Q8. Why standardize predictors before ridge or lasso? The penalty ∑βk2\sum \beta_k^2∑βk2​ or ∑∣βk∣\sum |\beta_k|∑∣βk​∣ adds up coefficients measured in each predictor’s own units. Without standardizing, a predictor measured in small units (and so carrying a large coefficient) would be penalized more than an identical predictor in large units. Standardizing puts every predictor on the same scale so the penalty is fair. Both glmnet and the scaled scikit-learn fit here do this.

12.8 Practice problems¶

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use fat.csv with response brozek and the thirteen predictors of this chapter, the full model of Example 12.1, and the eight-predictor candidate model (age, weight, neck, abdomen, hip, thigh, forearm, wrist). Recall the headline numbers: full R2=0.749R^2 = 0.749R2=0.749, full σ^2=15.9\hat{\sigma}^2 = 15.9σ^2=15.9, candidate Cp=6.19C_p = 6.19Cp​=6.19, candidate PRESS =4140= 4140=4140. Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in the instructor materials.

  1. (A) In one sentence each, say what multicollinearity does to the bias of bkb_kbk​, to the variance of bkb_kbk​, and to predictions Y^\hat{Y}Y^ made inside the data range. Then name two of the standard remedies for a predictor with a high VIF.

  2. (A) A predictor has VIF=5\mathrm{VIF} = 5VIF=5. What is the auxiliary Rk2R_k^2Rk2​, and how many times larger is Var⁡{bk}\operatorname{Var}\{b_k\}Var{bk​} than it would be if the predictor were uncorrelated with the others?

  3. (A) Explain why R2R^2R2 cannot be used to choose between a model with 5 predictors and one with 8 predictors, but adjusted R2R^2R2 can.

  4. (A) State the rule of thumb that connects a “good” model’s CpC_pCp​ to its number of parameters ppp, and say what a CpC_pCp​ far above ppp indicates.

  5. (A) Why does BIC tend to select smaller models than AIC? Point to the specific term that differs.

  6. (A) In plain words, what does the PRESS statistic measure that ordinary SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE does not?

  7. (A) A student selects predictors by stepwise regression, then reports the model’s ppp-values as evidence it is correct. Name the fallacy and explain it in two sentences.

  8. (A) Describe the difference between what ridge and lasso do to a coefficient, and say which one performs variable selection.

  9. (A) A model’s training RMSE is 3.5 and its 10-fold CV RMSE is 4.6. What does the gap tell you, and which number estimates performance on new data?

  10. (A) Explain why predictors are standardized before applying a ridge or lasso penalty.

  11. (B) Derive Var⁡{bk}=σ2/(Skk(1−Rk2))\operatorname{Var}\{b_k\} = \sigma^2/(S_{kk}(1 - R_k^2))Var{bk​}=σ2/(Skk​(1−Rk2​)) (Theorem 12.4) starting from the added-variable fact that bkb_kbk​ is the slope of YYY on the residuals of XkX_kXk​ regressed on the other predictors. Identify where VIFk\mathrm{VIF}_kVIFk​ appears.

  12. (B) Show that the auxiliary regression’s error sum of squares equals Skk(1−Rk2)S_{kk}(1 - R_k^2)Skk​(1−Rk2​), and use it to explain why a predictor nearly determined by the others has a nearly zero denominator in its coefficient variance.

  13. (B) Derive Mallows CpC_pCp​ (Theorem 12.5) from the standardized total mean squared error Γp\Gamma_pΓp​, including the steps ∑iVar⁡{Y^i}=pσ2\sum_i \operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_i\} = p\sigma^2∑i​Var{Y^i​}=pσ2 and E{SSEp}=B+(n−p)σ2E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} = B + (n - p)\sigma^2E{SSEp​}=B+(n−p)σ2. Conclude that an unbiased model has Cp≈pC_p \approx pCp​≈p.

  14. (B) Prove the PRESS deleted-residual shortcut Yi−Y^(i)=ei/(1−hii)Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = e_i/(1 - h_{ii})Yi​−Y^(i)​=ei​/(1−hii​) (Theorem 12.6), stating clearly where the Sherman-Morrison inverse update is used.

  15. (B) Show that for the full model, CpC_pCp​ equals its number of parameters exactly. (Start from the definition of CpC_pCp​ and the fact that σ^2\hat{\sigma}^2σ^2 is the full model’s MSE.)

  16. (B) A high-leverage case has hii=0.9h_{ii} = 0.9hii​=0.9 and ordinary residual ei=2e_i = 2ei​=2. Compute its deleted residual, and explain why leave-one-out prediction magnifies the residuals of high-leverage cases.

  17. (B) Explain, using the bias-variance decomposition of prediction error, why a model can have both lower training error and higher test error than a smaller model. Which term of the decomposition rises as the model grows?

  18. (B) Show that as λ→∞\lambda \to \inftyλ→∞ the ridge coefficient of a single standardized predictor β^ridge=∑xiYi/(∑xi2+λ)\hat{\beta}_{\text{ridge}} = \sum x_i Y_i / (\sum x_i^2 + \lambda)β^​ridge​=∑xi​Yi​/(∑xi2​+λ) tends to zero, and that at λ=0\lambda = 0λ=0 it equals the least-squares slope. (Use the one-predictor ridge solution, which you may derive in Problem 19.)

  19. (B) Derive the one-predictor ridge estimator in Problem 18 by setting the derivative of ∑(Yi−βxi)2+λβ2\sum (Y_i - \beta x_i)^2 + \lambda \beta^2∑(Yi​−βxi​)2+λβ2 to zero, and interpret the denominator as “signal plus penalty.”

  20. (C) Fit the full model and reproduce the VIFs. Identify every predictor with $\mathrm{VIF}

    10$ and, for one of them, confirm the VIF by running its auxiliary regression by hand.

  21. (C) Run the exhaustive best-subset search (R leaps, or the Python loop) and report the best model of each size with its CpC_pCp​. State the sizes chosen by CpC_pCp​, adjusted R2R^2R2, and BIC.

  22. (C) For the four-predictor best subset (weight, abdomen, forearm, wrist), compute CpC_pCp​, AIC, BIC, and PRESS. Compare each to the eight-predictor candidate and say which model you would report and why.

  23. (C) Write a function that computes PRESS from a fitted model using the shortcut, and verify it against a brute-force leave-one-out loop on the candidate model. Report both values.

  24. (C) Reproduce the pure-noise post-selection demonstration with a different seed. Report the selected model’s R2R^2R2 and overall FFF ppp-value, and explain why they are misleading.

  25. (C) Perform a seeded 70/30 train/test split. Fit the full and candidate models on the training set and report both test-set RMSEs. Which model predicts better, and does the order match the training-set fit?

  26. (C) Write a 5-fold cross-validation loop by hand and compare the full model, the eight-predictor candidate, and the four-predictor model (weight, abdomen, forearm, wrist) by CV RMSE. Rank them.

  27. (C) Fit a lasso with cv.glmnet (R) or LassoCV (Python) to the whole dataset. Report which predictors have nonzero coefficients at the cross-validated penalty, and compare that set to the eight-predictor CpC_pCp​ model.

  28. (C) Fit ridge regression across a grid of λ\lambdaλ values and plot the coefficient of weight against log⁡λ\log\lambdalogλ. Describe how the coefficient changes, and connect its behavior to the VIF of 33.5 you found for weight.

  29. (C) Using the candidate model, plot the deleted residuals ei/(1−hii)e_i/(1 - h_{ii})ei​/(1−hii​) against the ordinary residuals eie_iei​. Which cases move the most, and what property of those cases explains it?

  30. (C) Take the 25 percent of men with the largest leverages and refit the candidate model without them. Report how much the coefficients and PRESS change, and discuss whether the model is being propped up by a few cases.

12.9 Exam practice¶
